name: Enlil
shortDescription: Enlil was the Sumerian god of wind, air, storm, and earth. He was a chief deity in the Sumerian pantheon and a powerful force of both creation and destruction.
description:
Enlil, whose name means "Lord Wind," was a prominent deity in the Sumerian pantheon, considered one of the three most powerful gods alongside Anu (An) and Enki (Ea). He was the god of wind, air, storm, and earth, embodying both the benevolent forces of nature that allowed for agriculture and the destructive potential of storms and floods. Enlil was believed to be the son of Anshar and Kishar, primordial deities representing the horizons. His primary center of worship was the Ekur temple in Nippur, a city considered the religious center of Sumer. 

Enlil played a crucial role in Sumerian mythology, notably in the creation myth where he separated heaven (An) from earth (Ki), allowing for the world as we know it to exist. He is also a central figure in the story of the Great Flood. According to the Atrahasis epic and the Epic of Gilgamesh, Enlil, angered by the noise and overpopulation of humanity, decided to send a great flood to wipe them out. However, Enki, the god of wisdom, warned Atrahasis (or Utnapishtim in the Gilgamesh version), instructing him to build an ark and save himself, his family, and animals. Enlil's plan was thwarted, and he was later persuaded to find other, less drastic solutions to control the human population. Enlil's character is complex, representing a powerful but sometimes capricious and harsh force. He embodies the unpredictable nature of the elements and the challenges faced by early agricultural societies in controlling their environment. He was considered the ruler of the gods and was responsible for dispensing fates and granting kingship. His influence extended throughout Mesopotamian history, and he was later associated with the Akkadian god Enzu.

sources.title: The Electronic Text Corpus of Sumerian Literature
